title: K Closest Points to Origin (size-k max-heap)
code: static int[][] KClosest(int[][] points, int k)

frames.caption: Find the k = 2 points closest to the origin. Keep a size-k MAX-heap keyed by squared distance so the farthest of the current best sits at the root, ready to be evicted.
frames.note: Sorting all n points by distance is O(n log n). A size-k heap does better: we never keep more than k candidates, so each point costs O(log k) and the whole scan is O(n log k) time, O(k) space — the standard "top-k / k-closest" move.

frames.caption: Point [1,3]: squared distance d = 1² + 3² = 10.
frames.note: Priority = squared distance x*x + y*y, NOT the true Euclidean distance. Sqrt is monotonic, so ordering by x^2+y^2 gives the exact same "closest" ranking while staying in cheap, exact integer arithmetic — no Math.Sqrt call and no floating-point rounding error. This is the canonical reason "k closest" problems compare squared distances.

frames.caption: Heap has 0 < k = 2 points: enqueue [1,3] (d = 10). Farthest so far sits at the root: d = 10.
frames.note: While the heap holds fewer than k points we simply enqueue: we have not yet seen k candidates, so none can be ruled out. A MAX-heap keyed by distance keeps the FARTHEST of the current set at the root — the opposite of what we ultimately want to return, but exactly the element we may need to evict next. Each enqueue sifts up in O(log k).

frames.caption: Point [-2,2]: squared distance d = -2² + 2² = 8.
frames.note: Priority = squared distance x*x + y*y, NOT the true Euclidean distance. Sqrt is monotonic, so ordering by x^2+y^2 gives the exact same "closest" ranking while staying in cheap, exact integer arithmetic — no Math.Sqrt call and no floating-point rounding error. This is the canonical reason "k closest" problems compare squared distances.

frames.caption: Heap has 1 < k = 2 points: enqueue [-2,2] (d = 8). Farthest so far sits at the root: d = 10.
frames.note: While the heap holds fewer than k points we simply enqueue: we have not yet seen k candidates, so none can be ruled out. A MAX-heap keyed by distance keeps the FARTHEST of the current set at the root — the opposite of what we ultimately want to return, but exactly the element we may need to evict next. Each enqueue sifts up in O(log k).

frames.caption: Point [5,8]: squared distance d = 5² + 8² = 89.
frames.note: Priority = squared distance x*x + y*y, NOT the true Euclidean distance. Sqrt is monotonic, so ordering by x^2+y^2 gives the exact same "closest" ranking while staying in cheap, exact integer arithmetic — no Math.Sqrt call and no floating-point rounding error. This is the canonical reason "k closest" problems compare squared distances.

frames.caption: Heap full. Is [5,8] closer than the root (farthest kept), d = 89 < 10?
frames.note: The heap is full (size k). Its root is the farthest of the k points kept so far, so it is the one most likely to be beaten. Compare the new point against the root in O(1): if the newcomer is no closer than the current worst, it cannot belong in the answer and we discard it without touching the heap.

frames.caption: No: 89 ≥ 10. [5,8] is farther than the worst kept point — discard it (O(1)).
frames.note: The new point is farther than (or tied with) the heap root, the worst of the k already kept. Since k closer points already exist, this one can never be in the top-k, so we drop it untouched — an O(1) reject. Keeping only a size-k heap, not sorting all n points, is what makes this O(n log k) time and O(k) space.

frames.caption: Point [0,1]: squared distance d = 0² + 1² = 1.
frames.note: Priority = squared distance x*x + y*y, NOT the true Euclidean distance. Sqrt is monotonic, so ordering by x^2+y^2 gives the exact same "closest" ranking while staying in cheap, exact integer arithmetic — no Math.Sqrt call and no floating-point rounding error. This is the canonical reason "k closest" problems compare squared distances.

frames.caption: Heap full. Is [0,1] closer than the root (farthest kept), d = 1 < 10?
frames.note: The heap is full (size k). Its root is the farthest of the k points kept so far, so it is the one most likely to be beaten. Compare the new point against the root in O(1): if the newcomer is no closer than the current worst, it cannot belong in the answer and we discard it without touching the heap.

frames.caption: Yes: 1 < 10. Evict [1,3] (d = 10) and swap in [0,1]. New farthest at root: d = 8.
frames.note: The new point is closer than the current farthest (root), so it belongs among the k closest and the root does not. EnqueueDequeue swaps it in and pops the old root in one O(log k) sift — replacing the worst kept point with a better one. The heap again holds the k closest seen so far, with the new farthest bubbled to the root.

frames.caption: Scan complete. The heap holds the k = 2 closest points: [-2,2] and [0,1] — return them in any order.
frames.note: After one pass the heap contains exactly the k nearest points (order within the heap is irrelevant — the problem accepts any order). We never sorted the n points or kept more than k of them: O(n log k) time, O(k) space. Swap to a MIN-heap when you instead want the k FARthest.
